front: Play: one title character tells the other to leave her 'eternal gloves' in the kitchen, accusing her of wearing them to seduce a milkman
Play: a poisoned tea is prepared by the two protagonists, but the woman avoids it when she notices a phone is off the hook; the two protagonists love the milkman Mario
Play: at the end, one character describes rising from a woman's 'icy form' and proclaims 'We are beautiful, drunk, joyous, and free!'
Play: inspired by the case of Christine and Lea Papin; Solange and Claire take turns role-playing as Madame
Novel: the protagonist is beaten by rival Mimosa II, who shares her name with Mimosa I and Mimosa the half-IV
back: Our Lady of the Flowers (Jean Genet)

front: Novel: lovers are fictionalized under names like 'Seck Gorgui' and 'Darling Daintyfoot'; a character venerates twenty photos of murderers in his prison cell
back: Our Lady of the Flowers (Jean Genet)

front: Novel: the performer Harris Milstead took the stage name 'Divine' after the drag queen protagonist of this work, written in prison
back: Our Lady of the Flowers (Jean Genet)

front: Play: set in Irma's brothel, the 'House of Illusions,' where patrons dress as a judge, a bishop, and a general; the prostitute Chantal leads a revolution outside
Play: Said pursues Leila, 'the ugliest woman in town,' during an implied Algerian war of independence; satirizes colonizer Mr. Blankensee
Play: a Queen in whiteface presides over a kangaroo trial with an all-Black cast
Play: a beflowered coffin opens to reveal an old woman whom Deodatus Village claims to have strangled; the preface was fully rewritten
Author: Sartre wrote Saint Genet: Actor and Martyr about this author, structured around one 'conversion' and three 'metamorphoses'
Author: the one-armed Serb Stilitano breaks down crying in a hall of mirrors in this author's autobiographical The Thief's Journal
Novel: the rapist Harcamone dreams of his heart becoming a flower at the climax
back: The Miracle of the Rose (Jean Genet)

front: Author: only film features two prisoners who fantasize about frolicking in the countryside and blow smoke into each other's mouths
Author: Rainer Werner Fassbinder's final film adapted this author's novel about the sailor Querelle
Author: two of this author's plays inspired Todd Haynes's film Poison
